page: Amazon Web Services m1.xlarge is a first-generation general-purpose instance powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2651 v2 processor. It features 4 dedicated physical cores and 4 vCPUs running at a clock speed of 1.8 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. The instance is equipped with 15.0 GB of system memory, providing 3.75 GB per core, and utilizes the Xen hypervisor.

A key hardware characteristic of this instance is the inclusion of 1680 GB of local HDD storage. Networking capabilities are defined by a baseline bandwidth of 1 Gbps. No GPU accelerators are present on this instance type.

Benchmark data indicates that the m1.xlarge exhibits legacy performance profiles. Single-core and multi-core CPU benchmarks, such as stress-ng and Geekbench, place the instance in the bottom 10% to 25% of tested hardware. This is partly due to the lack of modern CPU instruction sets, including AVX, AVX512, and FMA. Memory bandwidth and database operations also score in the weak category, reflecting the older hardware architecture.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the m1.xlarge represents a legacy tier with low performance-per-resource density compared to modern cloud instances. It is best suited for legacy applications, basic file serving, or non-critical development workloads that require substantial local storage capacity but do not demand high-speed computational or I/O performance.
description: Amazon Web Services m1.xlarge is a legacy first-generation general-purpose instance featuring 4 dedicated vCPUs on an Intel Xeon E5-2651 v2 processor running at 1.8 GHz. Built on the x86_64 architecture and managed via the Xen hypervisor, it provides 15.0 GB of system memory and a substantial 1680 GB of local HDD storage. Performance benchmarks indicate a weak to poor profile, with single-core and multi-core CPU results falling into the bottom deciles due to the older processor architecture and lack of modern instruction sets like AVX or AVX512. The instance offers a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps and lacks GPU acceleration. Given its legacy hardware class and low performance-per-resource efficiency, the m1.xlarge is primarily suited for hosting legacy applications, basic file storage, or low-intensity background tasks that benefit from high local storage capacity without requiring high-speed compute or modern I/O performance.
og_description: Amazon Web Services m1.xlarge is a legacy general-purpose instance with 4 vCPUs, 15 GB RAM, and 1680 GB local HDD storage, offering basic performance for legacy workloads and file serving.

tagline: A legacy general-purpose instance featuring dedicated physical cores and high-capacity local HDD storage for basic, non-critical workloads.
